article.title: From Touchpoints to Transitions: A Journey-Centric Approach to Customer Analytics

editorial_activity.content:
KPIs such as bounce rates, sessions, pageviews were the foundation of a digital dashboard for nearly two decades, while in general KPIs are used according to the context of the analysis to measure and report performance, they only help us understand ‘what happened’ and not so much to understand ‘why users behave in certain ways or how business outcomes can be achieved’. As customer journeys have become increasingly complex and businesses are expected to deliver consistent performance improvements every passing quarter or year, the insights expected from data have increased in both volume and complexity. Analysts are expected to answer questions such as,

* Why did conversions drop despite an increase in traffic?

* Why do users abandon their carts even after adding shipping information?

* Why do our most loyal customers suddenly churn?

The question ‘why’ is deceptively simple and greatly influences the business and marketing strategy, but deriving meaningful insights is difficult. The aggregated metrics, KPIs, and dimension combinations that signal health often hide important behavioral nuances needed to understand and interpret the ‘why.’ The problem is not with the dashboarding platforms or the concept of KPIs. The questions business stakeholders ask have simply evolved beyond what dashboards can answer.

To effectively address business concerns and keep pace with evolving customer expectation, especially when Customer Experience (CX) has become far more fragmented across diverse channels, devices, and asynchronous interactions, the Analytics practice should evolve to look beyond traditional KPIs and structured dashboards toward a journey-centric analysis approach, I call it the Journey Explorer.

Journey Analysis as a concept is not new. Traditional analysis was focused only on ‘Progression’ with Funnel and Flow visualization, ignoring the critical causal factors of the journey, such as Friction, Detours, and Recovery. Let’s look at how applying these lenses on a simple Customer Journey on an Ecommerce site helps in getting better insights,

### **1. Progression: **It focuses on **“**Do** **users move forward”.

Image 1: [https://drive.google.com/file/d/17wvnnJux1ZXbe-dC_WdOanmgwy7NrXFQ](https://drive.google.com/file/d/17wvnnJux1ZXbe-dC_WdOanmgwy7NrXFQ/edit)

It tells us whether users are moving forward in the journey as designed or there are stages where users fall the most, signaling Friction at that journey stage, hampering users to move forward in the expected journey flow.

In the Ecommerce site analysis, this was at ‘View Item’ stage, the progression is only 66% and dropping over 30% of users in the journey, clearly there is a Friction at this stage which is affecting the user’s progression. At the same time ‘Add Shipping’ stage has the lowest Friction with only 10% of users who added their shipping information ended up not Purchasing, demonstrating high Progression rate, clear indication that designed\
experience is aligned with the user intent.

### 2. **Friction: **It focuses on “Where do Users stall?”.

Image 2: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1LaTEHs3hbnglmwUz0t4_J6k2aJTLBOdu/view?usp=drive_link>

Unlike bounce rate, which tells us where someone left, friction analysis tells us where the user hesitated, how long they stayed stuck, and whether they attempted the step multiple times before giving up.

As we saw in our Journey Explorer analysis, friction exists at ‘View Item’ stage related to Product View on the Ecommerce site. There can be many reasons for Friction that need to be diagnosed, the diagnosis can be effectively done by applying Micro-events, Time factor and other aspects depending on the context of Analysis.

Image 3: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1GQLBYUjfqxEf-G_ma0Y30FfI6SUJcwBN/view?usp=drive_link>

In our analysis the Friction was contributed by the errors and time taken by users to complete the action measured with Time distribution. The reason for longer time to action can be analyzed with Channel, Page, and Micro-Events at the stage. The Friction at this stage was also due to, most users are choosing ‘Add items to Wishlist’ rather than ‘Add to Cart’, although this is a favorable action it is not aligned to direct Journey Progression.

These are the actionable challenges that need to be resolved to remove Friction in user’s journey.

 

### 3. **Detour: **It focuses on “Where do Users go when they get lost?”.

Image 4: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1GF04Q_bKkezTBLPtYRLxtqP68wLWp28Y/view?usp=drive_link>

It is perhaps one of the most underutilized insights in traditional analytics. When users are not proceeding forward in journey, they either exit the site or take a detour to some other page which is not aligned with the designed journey. Detour analysis helps in mapping those alternate paths, revealing whether the users are seeking help, second-guessing their intent, or working around a broken experience. The detour paths often expose UX gaps that no survey or heatmap would surface.

In our analysis, we see users browsing FAQ and Shipping Policy pages at the critical Checkout process, although volume is low these detours could lead to Abandoned Cart journeys.

### 4. **Recovery: **It focuses on “Do Users come back?”.

Image 5: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1z3oXuAg9eqcA4-kjR3Qfgk-hKQ1BOPY4/view?usp=drive_link>

It helps in understanding if the users who detoured were a temporary distraction or break in the journey. Behavior of users who detoured and returned is distinctly different from the one who never left, as they demonstrated intent but got distracted. Recovery rates, analyzed alongside the detour paths give analysts a direct measure of experience resilience, how forgiving is the journey when something goes wrong?

In our analysis, only 38% of the users who detoured recovered back in the journey suggesting a need to enhance the journey experience to improve their resilience.

As an additional lens, consider filtering the Journey Explorer based on Cohorts and Audience Segments.

### 5. **Cohort based Journey Analysis:**

Journey on any website/app is not uniform across all the users, there are inherent Cohorts of users who would interact with the website/app differently, having the ability to view Journey Exploration across all users and when needed filtering by predefined cohorts give a better understanding of the change in Journey Factors and Performance across Cohorts.

To get additional perspectives and insights on each of the focus areas seen above, we can look at stage specific contributors such as,

* **Pages or Top paths:** It helps in understanding what are the top contributing pages at the stage, these pages play critical influencing role on user’s action, any page which is expected and not present in the list should be analyzed and optimized to improve the performance.

Image 6: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1JZzUCXxIelFJHiy1NmYuaWSLV6n-YJ42/view?usp=drive_link>

* **Channels:** The top traffic source view at the journey stage helps in understanding if the volumes at a particular journey stage is greatly influenced by internal site navigation and finding methods or driven by the marketing campaigns

Image 7: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1x4rQ2eJj3Uzq3XabePaitOcUsHCfYdHf/view?usp=drive_link>

* **Micro-events:** As we already saw at ‘View Item’ stage while understanding Friction, Micro-events are greatly helpful in understanding if the actions performed by the users are aligned directly to the Journey progression or contributing to Friction although still favorable, for instance the ‘Add to Wishlist’ action instead of ‘Add to Cart’ action which drives the real business goal.

editorial_activity.content:
KPIs such as bounce rates, sessions, pageviews were the foundation of a digital dashboard for nearly two decades, while in general KPIs are used according to the context of the analysis to measure and report performance, they only help us understand ‘what happened’ and not so much to understand ‘why users behave in certain ways or how business outcomes can be achieved’. As customer journeys have become increasingly complex and businesses are expected to deliver consistent performance improvements every passing quarter or year, the insights expected from data have increased in both volume and complexity. Analysts are expected to answer questions such as,

* Why did conversions drop despite an increase in traffic?

* Why do users abandon their carts even after adding shipping information?

* Why do our most loyal customers suddenly churn?

The question ‘why’ is deceptively simple and greatly influences the business and marketing strategy, but deriving meaningful insights is difficult. The aggregated metrics, KPIs, and dimension combinations that signal health often hide important behavioral nuances needed to understand and interpret the ‘why.’ The problem is not with the dashboarding platforms or the concept of KPIs. The questions business stakeholders ask have simply evolved beyond what dashboards can answer.

To effectively address business concerns and keep pace with evolving customer expectation, especially when Customer Experience (CX) has become far more fragmented across diverse channels, devices, and asynchronous interactions, the Analytics practice should evolve to look beyond traditional KPIs and structured dashboards toward a journey-centric analysis approach, I call it the Journey Explorer.

Journey Analysis as a concept is not new. Traditional analysis was focused only on ‘Progression’ with Funnel and Flow visualization, ignoring the critical causal factors of the journey, such as Friction, Detours, and Recovery. Let’s look at how applying these lenses on a simple Customer Journey on an Ecommerce site helps in getting better insights,

### **1. Progression: **It focuses on **“**Do** **users move forward”.

Image 1: [https://drive.google.com/file/d/17wvnnJux1ZXbe-dC_WdOanmgwy7NrXFQ](https://drive.google.com/file/d/17wvnnJux1ZXbe-dC_WdOanmgwy7NrXFQ/edit)

It tells us whether users are moving forward in the journey as designed or there are stages where users fall the most, signaling Friction at that journey stage, hampering users to move forward in the expected journey flow.

In the Ecommerce site analysis, this was at ‘View Item’ stage, the progression is only 66% and dropping over 30% of users in the journey, clearly there is a Friction at this stage which is affecting the user’s progression. At the same time ‘Add Shipping’ stage has the lowest Friction with only 10% of users who added their shipping information ended up not Purchasing, demonstrating high Progression rate, clear indication that designed experience is aligned with the user intent.

### 2. **Friction: **It focuses on “Where do Users stall?”.

Image 2: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1LaTEHs3hbnglmwUz0t4_J6k2aJTLBOdu/view?usp=drive_link>

Unlike bounce rate, which tells us where someone left, friction analysis tells us where the user hesitated, how long they stayed stuck, and whether they attempted the step multiple times before giving up.

As we saw in our Journey Explorer analysis, friction exists at ‘View Item’ stage related to Product View on the Ecommerce site. There can be many reasons for Friction that need to be diagnosed, the diagnosis can be effectively done by applying Micro-events, Time factor and other aspects depending on the context of Analysis.

Image 3: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1GQLBYUjfqxEf-G_ma0Y30FfI6SUJcwBN/view?usp=drive_link>

In our analysis the Friction was contributed by the errors and time taken by users to complete the action measured with Time distribution. The reason for longer time to action can be analyzed with Channel, Page, and Micro-Events at the stage. The Friction at this stage was also due to, most users are choosing ‘Add items to Wishlist’ rather than ‘Add to Cart’, although this is a favorable action it is not aligned to direct Journey Progression.

These are the actionable challenges that need to be resolved to remove Friction in user’s journey.

 

### 3. **Detour: **It focuses on “Where do Users go when they get lost?”.

Image 4: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1GF04Q_bKkezTBLPtYRLxtqP68wLWp28Y/view?usp=drive_link>

It is perhaps one of the most underutilized insights in traditional analytics. When users are not proceeding forward in journey, they either exit the site or take a detour to some other page which is not aligned with the designed journey. Detour analysis helps in mapping those alternate paths, revealing whether the users are seeking help, second-guessing their intent, or working around a broken experience. The detour paths often expose UX gaps that no survey or heatmap would surface.

In our analysis, we see users browsing FAQ and Shipping Policy pages at the critical Checkout process, although volume is low these detours could lead to Abandoned Cart journeys.

### 4. **Recovery: **It focuses on “Do Users come back?”.

Image 5: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1z3oXuAg9eqcA4-kjR3Qfgk-hKQ1BOPY4/view?usp=drive_link>

It helps in understanding if the users who detoured were a temporary distraction or break in the journey. Behavior of users who detoured and returned is distinctly different from the one who never left, as they demonstrated intent but got distracted. Recovery rates, analyzed alongside the detour paths give analysts a direct measure of experience resilience, how forgiving is the journey when something goes wrong?

In our analysis, only 38% of the users who detoured recovered back in the journey suggesting a need to enhance the journey experience to improve their resilience.

As an additional lens, consider filtering the Journey Explorer based on Cohorts and Audience Segments.

### 5. **Cohort based Journey Analysis:**

Journey on any website/app is not uniform across all the users, there are inherent Cohorts of users who would interact with the website/app differently, having the ability to view Journey Exploration across all users and when needed filtering by predefined cohorts give a better understanding of the change in Journey Factors and Performance across Cohorts.

To get additional perspectives and insights on each of the focus areas seen above, we can look at stage specific contributors such as,

* **Pages or Top paths:** It helps in understanding what are the top contributing pages at the stage, these pages play critical influencing role on user’s action, any page which is expected and not present in the list should be analyzed and optimized to improve the performance.

Image 6: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1JZzUCXxIelFJHiy1NmYuaWSLV6n-YJ42/view?usp=drive_link>

* **Channels:** The top traffic source view at the journey stage helps in understanding if the volumes at a particular journey stage is greatly influenced by internal site navigation and finding methods or driven by the marketing campaigns

Image 7: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1x4rQ2eJj3Uzq3XabePaitOcUsHCfYdHf/view?usp=drive_link>

* **Micro-events:** As we already saw at ‘View Item’ stage while understanding Friction, Micro-events are greatly helpful in understanding if the actions performed by the users are aligned directly to the Journey progression or contributing to Friction although still favorable, for instance the ‘Add to Wishlist’ action instead of ‘Add to Cart’ action which drives the real business goal.

### Conclusion

Journey Explorer can represent a meaningful approach for analysts to conduct customer journey analysis. By shifting the unit of analysis from just pages to transitions in the journey, it brings into focus what aggregate metrics have obscured, the actual experience of the users moving through the journey, making decisions, hitting walls, taking detours, and sometimes recovering to find their way back.\
\
No single lens makes the Journey Explorer powerful. It is the combination of lenses along with cohorts and their contributing factors. Progression establishes the baseline. Friction pinpoints where intervention is needed. Detour reveals the path users take when the designed journey fails them. Recovery measures how well the experience holds under real-world conditions. Cohort filtering ensures these insights are specific rather than averaged across all users, because behavior that looks uniform at aggregate data level is rarely uniform in practice.

For analysts, Journey Explorer is an additional layer and not a replacement for dashboards, it answers the questions the dashboards surface most of the time but cannot resolve them independently. editorial_activity.comment:
**Coherence:** 7/10
The article follows a logical structural arc from problem identification to framework introduction to component breakdown, and the four-lens structure (Progression, Friction, Detour, Recovery) gives the piece a clear skeleton. However, the opening paragraph is syntactically overloaded and disrupts early momentum; the transition into "Journey Explorer" as a named framework feels abrupt without a proper definitional anchor before the framework is deployed.

**Relevance:** 8/10
The content is tightly scoped to analytics practitioners working in digital and ecommerce contexts, and the questions posed at the outset ("Why did conversions drop despite an increase in traffic?") resonate directly with the target audience's lived professional experience. The addition of cohort filtering and micro-events elevates relevance beyond surface-level content. A brief acknowledgment of how this framework intersects with existing tools (GA4, Adobe, CDP platforms) would sharpen practitioner applicability.

**Accuracy:** 7/10
The conceptual definitions of Friction, Detour, and Recovery are analytically sound and correctly distinguished from conventional funnel metrics. The Wishlist-versus-Cart distinction as a friction signal is a genuinely accurate behavioral observation. However, the claim that "no survey or heatmap would surface" detour paths is an overstatement — session replay and heatmap tools do capture off-path navigation to some degree, and this assertion weakens analytical credibility.

**Readability:** 6/10
The prose is readable in sections but inconsistent throughout. The opening paragraph is a run-on construction that will lose readers before the framework is introduced. Capitalization of terms like Friction, Detour, Recovery, Progression, and Cohort is applied inconsistently and without explanation, creating a stylistic oddity that reads as unpolished rather than intentional. Sentence length varies appropriately in the body, but several transitions are clipped and underdeveloped.

**Originality:** 7/10
The "Journey Explorer" framing — particularly the four-lens model that extends traditional funnel analysis — represents a genuine conceptual contribution rather than a repackaging of standard analytics content. The Detour and Recovery lenses are underrepresented in mainstream analytics discourse, and naming them explicitly adds value. The article does not exhibit the hallmarks of heavy AI generation: sentence rhythm varies, the examples are specific rather than generic, and there are no filler clichés of the "in today's fast-paced world" variety. The writing reads as human-authored with some structural roughness consistent with that assessment.

**Grammar:** 5/10
This is the article's most significant technical weakness. The opening paragraph is a structurally malformed run-on. Commas are used in place of periods or semicolons in multiple instances ("...I call it the Journey Explorer" is appended without proper punctuation separation). Capitalization of common nouns (Friction, Detour, Cohort) is inconsistent across sections. Phrases such as "the one who never left" and "most users are choosing" reflect informal grammatical construction inappropriate for a professional publication. A full copy-edit pass is mandatory before publication.

**Tone:** 7/10
The article carries a practitioner's voice throughout, and the first-person ownership of the Journey Explorer framework ("I call it") lends credibility and authority. The tone does not inflate claims unnecessarily and avoids hype language. Occasional informality ("Let's look at how") is borderline for this publication's standards but does not critically undermine the professional register.

**Completeness:** 6/10
The framework is introduced and its components are explained with ecommerce examples, but the article stops short of telling analysts *how* to implement this approach. There is no guidance on data infrastructure requirements, event taxonomy design, tooling considerations, or how to handle cross-device and cross-session journeys — all of which are material concerns for practitioners attempting to apply this in practice. The Cohort section (Section 5) is noticeably thin compared to the four core lenses and reads as an afterthought. The conclusion is strong but cannot compensate for the implementation gap in the body.

**Title and Hook:** 6/10
The title is the article's strongest single element — "From Touchpoints to Transitions" is precise, evocative, and correctly signals a conceptual shift rather than a listicle. The opening paragraph, however, fails to honor the title's promise. It front-loads a dense clause about legacy KPIs without a scene-setting statement, a concrete scenario, or a tension that pulls the reader forward. The hook needs to be rebuilt as a focused, punchy opening that earns the structural argument that follows.

---

**AI-Generation Index:** 12%
**Overall:** 7/10
The article's primary strength is its conceptual framework: the four-lens Journey Explorer model is analytically coherent, meaningfully differentiated from conventional funnel analysis, and communicated with practitioner specificity. The Detour and Recovery lenses in particular represent underexplored territory that adds genuine value to the publication's audience. However, the article is held back by a grammatically deficient opening, inconsistent capitalization conventions that signal unfinished editing, an overstatement regarding heatmap limitations, and a shallow implementation section that leaves practitioners without actionable next steps. The article meets the 7/10 threshold for conditional approval but must undergo a mandatory copy-edit pass, a reconstructed opening paragraph, and an expanded Section 5 before it is suitable for publication.

Image 1: [https://drive.google.com/file/d/17wvnnJux1ZXbe-dC_WdOanmgwy7NrXFQ](https://drive.google.com/file/d/17wvnnJux1ZXbe-dC_WdOanmgwy7NrXFQ/edit)

It tells us whether users are moving forward in the journey as designed or there are stages where users fall the most, signaling friction at that journey stage, hampering users to move forward in the expected journey flow.

In the Ecommerce site analysis, this was at ‘View Item’ stage, the progression is only 66% and dropping over 30% of users in the journey, clearly there is a friction at this stage which is affecting the user’s progression. At the same time ‘Add Shipping’ stage has the lowest friction with only 10% of users who added their shipping information ended up not Purchasing, demonstrating high Progression rate, clear indication that designed experience is aligned with the user intent.

### 2. **Friction: **It focuses on “Where do Users stall?”.

Image 2: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1LaTEHs3hbnglmwUz0t4_J6k2aJTLBOdu/view?usp=drive_link>

Unlike bounce rate, which tells us where someone left, friction analysis tells us where the user hesitated, how long they stayed stuck, and whether they attempted the step multiple times before giving up.

As we saw in our Journey Explorer analysis, friction exists at ‘View Item’ stage related to Product View on the Ecommerce site. There can be many reasons for friction that need to be diagnosed, the diagnosis can be effectively done by applying Micro-events, Time factor and other aspects depending on the context of Analysis.

Image 3: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1GQLBYUjfqxEf-G_ma0Y30FfI6SUJcwBN/view?usp=drive_link>

In our analysis the friction was contributed by the errors and time taken by users to complete the action measured with Time distribution. The reason for longer time to action can be analyzed with Channel, Page, and Micro-Events at the stage. The friction at this stage was also due to, most users chose ‘Add items to Wishlist’ rather than ‘Add to Cart’, although this is a favorable action it is not aligned to direct Journey Progression.

These are the actionable challenges that need to be resolved to remove friction in the user’s journey.

 

### 3. **Detour: **It focuses on “Where do Users go when they get lost?”.

Image 4: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1GF04Q_bKkezTBLPtYRLxtqP68wLWp28Y/view?usp=drive_link>

It is perhaps one of the most underutilized insights in traditional analytics. When users are not proceeding forward in journey, they either exit the site or take a detour to some other page which is not aligned with the designed journey. Detour analysis helps in mapping those alternate paths, revealing whether the users are seeking help, second-guessing their intent, or working around a broken experience. The detour paths often expose UX gaps that surveys or heatmaps rarely surface.

In our analysis, we see users browsing FAQ and Shipping Policy pages at the critical Checkout process, although volume is low these detours could lead to Abandoned Cart journeys.

### 4. **Recovery: **It focuses on “Do Users come back?”.

Image 5: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1z3oXuAg9eqcA4-kjR3Qfgk-hKQ1BOPY4/view?usp=drive_link>

It helps in understanding if the users who detoured were a temporary distraction or break in the journey. Behavior of users who detoured and returned is distinctly different from the one who never left, as they demonstrated intent but got distracted. Recovery rates, analyzed alongside the detour paths give analysts a direct measure of experience resilience, how forgiving is the journey when something goes wrong?

In our analysis, only 38% of the users who detoured recovered back in the journey suggesting a need to enhance the journey experience to improve their resilience.

Along with the 4 lenses, these additional contributors help better understand the customer journey.

### **Cohorts:**

All users do not follow the same path or journey. There are inherent differences between user journeys, it could be caused due to the device they are using, region, gender etc. These differences can be defined as cohorts of the users. Applying these cohorts to slice the aggregate journey at an individual cohort level to identify cohort level 'Progression,' 'Friction,' 'Detour' and 'Recovery'. These lenses give a better understanding of cohort or segment level performance.

### Stage-Level Contributors:

To get additional perspective and insights on each of the lenses at an individual stage, we can identify diagnostic contributors to further analyze the behavior, in my analysis for Ecommerce the following contributors were applied,

* **Pages or Top paths:** It helps in understanding what are the top contributing pages at the stage, these pages play critical influencing role on user’s action, any page which is expected and not present in the list should be analyzed and optimized to improve the performance.

Image 6: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1JZzUCXxIelFJHiy1NmYuaWSLV6n-YJ42/view?usp=drive_link>

* **Channels:** The top traffic source view at the journey stage helps in understanding if the volumes at a particular journey stage is greatly influenced by internal site navigation and finding methods or driven by the marketing campaigns

Image 7: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1x4rQ2eJj3Uzq3XabePaitOcUsHCfYdHf/view?usp=drive_link>

* **Micro-events:** As we already saw at ‘View Item’ stage while understanding Friction, Micro-events are greatly helpful in understanding if the actions performed by the users are aligned directly to the Journey progression or contributing to Friction although still favorable, for instance the ‘Add to Wishlist’ action instead of ‘Add to Cart’ action which drives the real business goal.

### Conclusion

Journey Explorer can represent a meaningful approach for analysts to conduct customer journey analysis. By shifting the unit of analysis from just pages to transitions in the journey, it brings into focus what aggregate metrics have obscured, the actual experience of the users moving through the journey, making decisions, hitting walls, taking detours, and sometimes recovering to find their way back.\
\
No single lens makes the Journey Explorer powerful. It is the combination of lenses along with cohorts and their contributing factors. Progression establishes the baseline. Friction pinpoints where intervention is needed. Detour reveals the path users take when the designed journey fails them. Recovery measures how well the experience holds under real-world conditions. Cohort filtering ensures these insights are specific rather than averaged across all users, because behavior that looks uniform at aggregate data level is rarely uniform in practice.

For analysts, Journey Explorer is an additional layer and not a replacement for dashboards, it answers the questions the dashboards surface most of the time but cannot resolve them independently. When a KPI drops, Journey Explorer tells us where the drop originated from in the journey, what did the users do instead, and whether the experience recovered. That is the difference between monitoring performance and understanding the journey.

editorial_activity.comment:
**Coherence:** 7/10
The article follows a logical structure, moving from the limitations of KPIs to the four-lens framework with reasonable consistency. However, the transition between the introductory critique of dashboards and the Journey Explorer framework feels abrupt — the concept is introduced with "I call it the Journey Explorer" without adequate scaffolding or definition before the reader is thrust into Progression analysis.

**Relevance:** 8/10
The subject matter is squarely within the analytics and digital intelligence domain and addresses a genuine practitioner pain point. The e-commerce use case grounds the framework in a recognizable business context, making it accessible to the target audience without deviating into irrelevant territory.

**Accuracy:** 7/10
The conceptual distinction between Progression, Friction, Detour, and Recovery is analytically sound and reflects legitimate behavioral analytics thinking. However, the article makes no reference to established methodologies such as process mining, customer journey analytics platforms, or behavioral cohort analysis literature — missing an opportunity to substantiate the framework's rigor and distinguish it from existing practice.

**Readability:** 6/10
Sentence construction is uneven throughout. Several sentences are grammatically labored or structurally ambiguous (e.g., "Behavior of users who detoured and returned is distinctly different from the one who never left"). The reliance on image placeholders that are inaccessible to the reader creates significant dead zones in the narrative — the article cannot stand on its own without those visuals, which is a critical editorial deficiency.

**Originality:** 7/10
The four-lens framing is a genuinely useful synthesis that goes beyond standard funnel analysis commentary, and the "Journey Explorer" positioning shows authorial intent. The writing cadence is human and imperfect in ways that suggest authentic authorship — there is no detectable over-reliance on AI phrasing patterns. The framework itself, however, borrows conceptually from behavioral analytics and UX research traditions without acknowledging them, which limits the perception of originality.

**Grammar:** 5/10
There are recurring grammatical errors that would be unacceptable in a publication of this standard. Notable issues include: missing articles ("customer expectation" should be "customer expectations"), comma splices, inconsistent capitalization of analytical terms mid-article, and fragmented constructions such as "it could be caused due to the device they are using, region, gender etc." The closing section improves in precision, but the cumulative errors in the body are significant.

**Tone:** 7/10
The voice is generally authoritative and practitioner-focused, avoiding both excessive technicality and excessive simplification. The occasional switch to first-person ("I call it the Journey Explorer," "in my analysis") is not inherently problematic but is applied inconsistently — the article would benefit from a committed decision on whether this is a personal opinion piece or a structured methodology presentation.

**Completeness:** 6/10
The four lenses are introduced and illustrated but not fully developed. The Cohorts and Stage-Level Contributors sections feel appended rather than integrated into the core framework. There is no discussion of data requirements, tooling considerations, implementation challenges, or limitations of the Journey Explorer approach — all of which a senior analytics practitioner would immediately ask. The article identifies the "what" of the framework but underdelivers on the "how."

**Title and Hook:** 6/10
The title is genuinely strong — "From Touchpoints to Transitions" is precise and signals a meaningful conceptual shift. The opening paragraph, however, squanders that momentum. Leading with a list of three familiar KPIs and a generic statement about dashboards is a missed opportunity. A practitioner reading this publication will not be surprised by the observation that bounce rates fail to explain "why" — the hook needs to earn the reader's attention, not state the obvious.

---

**AI-Generation Index:** 12%
**Overall:** 6/10
The article presents a coherent and practitioner-relevant framework that shows genuine analytical thinking, and the "Journey Explorer" lens structure is a useful contribution to the conversation around behavioral analytics. However, the piece fails to meet the publication threshold for three compounding reasons: the grammar errors are too frequent and too elementary for a senior editorial standard; the completeness deficit leaves the framework feeling like a concept sketch rather than a rigorous methodology; and the inaccessible image references render critical sections of the argument inert for any reader without privileged file access. The article requires substantive revision — grammatical correction throughout, full integration of visuals or self-sufficient prose replacements, a stronger opening hook, and an expanded treatment of the framework's implementation logic and limitations — before it is suitable for publication.

Image 4: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1GF04Q_bKkezTBLPtYRLxtqP68wLWp28Y/view?usp=drive_link>

It is perhaps one of the most underutilized insights in traditional analytics. When users are not proceeding forward in journey, they either exit the site or take a detour to some other page which is not aligned with the designed journey. Detour analysis helps in mapping those alternate paths, revealing whether the users are seeking help, second-guessing their intent, or working around a broken experience. The detour paths often expose UX gaps that surveys or heatmaps rarely surface.

In our analysis, we see users browsing FAQ and Shipping Policy pages at the critical Checkout process, although volume is low these detours could lead to Abandoned Cart journeys.

### 4. **Recovery: **It focuses on “Do Users come back?”.

Image 5: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1z3oXuAg9eqcA4-kjR3Qfgk-hKQ1BOPY4/view?usp=drive_link>

It helps in understanding if the users who detoured were a temporary distraction or break in the journey. Behavior of users who detoured and returned is distinctly different from the one who never left, as they demonstrated intent but got distracted. Recovery rates, analyzed alongside the detour paths give analysts a direct measure of experience resilience, how forgiving is the journey when something goes wrong?

In our analysis, only 38% of the users who detoured recovered back in the journey suggesting a need to enhance the journey experience to improve their resilience.

Along with the 4 lenses, these additional contributors help better understand the customer journey.

### **Cohorts:**

All users do not follow the same path or journey. There are inherent differences between user journeys, it could be caused due to the device they are using, region, gender etc. These differences can be defined as cohorts of the users. Applying these cohorts to slice the aggregate journey at an individual cohort level to identify cohort level 'Progression,' 'Friction,' 'Detour' and 'Recovery'. These lenses give a better understanding of cohort or segment level performance.

### Stage-Level Contributors:

To get additional perspective and insights on each of the lenses at an individual stage, we can identify diagnostic contributors to further analyze the behavior, in my analysis for Ecommerce the following contributors were applied,

* **Pages or Top paths:** It helps in understanding what are the top contributing pages at the stage, these pages play critical influencing role on user’s action, any page which is expected and not present in the list should be analyzed and optimized to improve the performance.

Image 6: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1JZzUCXxIelFJHiy1NmYuaWSLV6n-YJ42/view?usp=drive_link>

* **Channels:** The top traffic source view at the journey stage helps in understanding if the volumes at a particular journey stage is greatly influenced by internal site navigation and finding methods or driven by the marketing campaigns

Image 7: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1x4rQ2eJj3Uzq3XabePaitOcUsHCfYdHf/view?usp=drive_link>

* **Micro-events:** As we already saw at ‘View Item’ stage while understanding Friction, Micro-events are greatly helpful in understanding if the actions performed by the users are aligned directly to the Journey progression or contributing to Friction although still favorable, for instance the ‘Add to Wishlist’ action instead of ‘Add to Cart’ action which drives the real business goal.

### Conclusion

Journey Explorer can represent a meaningful approach for analysts to conduct customer journey analysis. By shifting the unit of analysis from just pages to transitions in the journey, it brings into focus what aggregate metrics have obscured, the actual experience of the users moving through the journey, making decisions, hitting walls, taking detours, and sometimes recovering to find their way back.\
\
No single lens makes the Journey Explorer powerful. It is the combination of lenses along with cohorts and their contributing factors. Progression establishes the baseline. Friction pinpoints where intervention is needed. Detour reveals the path users take when the designed journey fails them. Recovery measures how well the experience holds under real-world conditions. Cohort filtering ensures these insights are specific rather than averaged across all users, because behavior that looks uniform at aggregate data level is rarely uniform in practice.

For analysts, Journey Explorer is an additional layer and not a replacement for dashboards, it answers the questions the dashboards surface most of the time but cannot resolve them independently. When a KPI drops, Journey Explorer tells us where the drop originated from in the journey, what did the users do instead, and whether the experience recovered. That is the difference between monitoring performance and understanding the journey.
editorial_activity.comment:
**Coherence:** 7/10
The article follows a logical structure, moving from problem statement to framework introduction to component breakdown. However, the transition between the four lenses and the "Stage-Level Contributors" section feels abrupt and under-signposted, disrupting what is otherwise a serviceable narrative arc. A brief bridging paragraph explaining how contributors relate structurally to the four lenses would resolve this.

**Relevance:** 8/10
The content is well-calibrated to analytics practitioners working in digital and ecommerce contexts. The framing around KPI limitations, funnel analysis, and behavioral diagnosis speaks directly to practitioner pain points. The article would benefit from briefly acknowledging applicability beyond ecommerce, as the framework's scope appears broader than the single use case presented.

**Accuracy:** 7/10
The conceptual definitions of Friction, Detour, and Recovery are defensible and reasonably precise. The claim that friction analysis reveals "how long they stayed stuck" and "whether they attempted the step multiple times" is accurate but asserted without explaining which data signals or event structures enable this measurement, which weakens analytical credibility. A brief technical grounding would strengthen these claims considerably.

**Readability:** 6/10
The prose is generally accessible, but several sentences are structurally overloaded and require re-reading (e.g., "The aggregated metrics, KPIs, and dimension combinations that signal health often hide important behavioral nuances needed to understand and interpret the 'why.'"). Bullet points and section headers do useful work, but the inline image references to external Google Drive links are a significant readability failure — they break immersion entirely and are unsuitable for publication. All visuals must be embedded or properly captioned.

**Originality:** 7/10
The "Journey Explorer" framing — specifically the four-lens model of Progression, Friction, Detour, and Recovery — is a genuinely differentiated contribution relative to standard funnel analysis discourse. The article does not feel AI-generated; it carries a distinct practitioner voice with first-person ownership and applied specificity. However, the opening paragraphs rehearse well-worn critiques of KPI-centric dashboards that add little new ground before the framework emerges.

**Grammar:** 6/10
There are recurring grammatical issues that require editorial intervention. Subject-verb agreement errors appear ("most users chose 'Add items to Wishlist' rather than 'Add to Cart', although this is a favorable action it is not aligned"), comma splices are present throughout, and the phrase "fall the most" (likely intended as "drop off the most") reads as unpolished. The sentence "It tells us whether users are moving forward in the journey as designed or there are stages where users fall the most" needs restructuring. A thorough copyedit pass is mandatory before publication.

**Tone:** 7/10
The voice is practitioner-authoritative and avoids both excessive technicality and populist oversimplification. The first-person ownership of the framework ("I call it the Journey Explorer") is appropriate and adds credibility. However, tone becomes slightly informal and imprecise in places — "hitting walls" and "getting lost" in the conclusion weaken the otherwise professional register.

**Completeness:** 6/10
The framework is introduced and illustrated, but critical gaps remain. There is no discussion of data prerequisites — what event taxonomy, instrumentation, or tooling is required to operationalize the Journey Explorer. There is no mention of limitations, edge cases, or failure modes of the framework. The Cohorts section is disproportionately thin relative to the other lenses and reads as a late addition rather than a fully developed component. These omissions prevent the article from functioning as actionable guidance rather than conceptual advocacy.

**Title and Hook:** 6/10
The title is strong — "From Touchpoints to Transitions" signals a meaningful conceptual shift and will resonate with analytics readers. However, the opening paragraph immediately retreats into a dry enumeration of legacy KPIs and generic critique, squandering the momentum the title generates. The hook should lead with the practitioner's lived frustration or a concrete scenario, not a definitional preamble about what KPIs are used for.

---

**AI-Generation Index:** 12%
**Overall:** 7/10
The article's primary strength is the Journey Explorer framework itself — the four-lens model is a coherent, practitioner-grounded contribution that meaningfully extends beyond conventional funnel analysis. However, the piece is undermined by three material issues: the Google Drive image links are categorically unpublishable in their current form and must be replaced with embedded assets; grammatical errors are frequent enough to require a full copyedit before submission; and the Completeness gap around instrumentation and tooling requirements leaves analytically sophisticated readers without a path to implementation. The article clears the 7/10 threshold for conditional approval, but publication must be contingent on resolution of the image embedding issue, a mandatory copyedit pass, and expansion of the Completeness gaps identified above. These are non-negotiable pre-publication requirements.

editorial_activity.content:
KPIs such as bounce rates, sessions, pageviews were the foundation of a digital dashboard for nearly two decades. In general, KPIs are used according to the context of the analysis to measure and report performance. However, they only help us understand ‘what happened’ and not so much to understand ‘why users behave in certain ways or how business outcomes can be achieved.’ As customer journeys have become increasingly complex and businesses are expected to deliver consistent performance improvements every passing quarter or year, the insights expected from data have increased in both volume and complexity. Analysts are expected to answer questions such as,

* Why did conversions drop despite an increase in traffic?

* Why do users abandon their carts even after adding shipping information?

* Why do our most loyal customers suddenly churn?

The question ‘why’ is deceptively simple and greatly influences the business and marketing strategy, but deriving meaningful insights is difficult. The aggregated metrics, KPIs, and dimension combinations that signal health often hide important behavioral nuances needed to understand and interpret the ‘why.’ The problem is not with the dashboarding platforms or the concept of KPIs. The questions business stakeholders ask have simply evolved beyond what dashboards can answer.

To effectively address business concerns and keep pace with evolving customer expectation, especially when Customer Experience (CX) has become far more fragmented across diverse channels, devices, and asynchronous interactions, the Analytics practice should evolve to look beyond traditional KPIs and structured dashboards toward a journey-centric analysis approach, I call it the Journey Explorer. Here is a working reference Journey Explorer implementation for an Ecommerce site, it can be explored at <https://devyendarm.github.io/JourneyExplorer>.

Journey Analysis as a concept is not new. It tells us whether users are moving forward in the journey as designed or there are stages where users fall the most, signaling friction at that journey stage, hampering users to move forward in the expected journey flow.

In the Ecommerce site analysis, this was at ‘View Item’ stage, the progression is only 66% and dropping over 30% of users in the journey, clearly there is a friction at this stage which is affecting the user’s progression. At the same time ‘Add Shipping’ stage has the lowest friction with only 10% of users who added their shipping information ended up not Purchasing, demonstrating high Progression rate, clear indication that designed experience is aligned with the user intent.

### 2. **Friction: **It focuses on “Where do Users stall?”.

As we saw in our Journey Explorer analysis, friction exists at ‘View Item’ stage related to Product View on the Ecommerce site. There can be many reasons for friction that need to be diagnosed, the diagnosis can be effectively done by applying Micro-events, Time factor and other aspects depending on the context of Analysis.

Image 3: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1GQLBYUjfqxEf-G_ma0Y30FfI6SUJcwBN/view?usp=drive_link>

In our analysis the friction was contributed by the errors and time taken by users to complete the action measured with Time distribution. The reason for longer time to action can be analyzed with Channel, Page, and Micro-Events at the stage. The friction at this stage was also due to, most users chose ‘Add items to Wishlist’ rather than ‘Add to Cart’, although this is a favorable action it is not aligned to direct Journey Progression.

### 4. **Recovery: **It focuses on “Do Users come back?”.

Image 5: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1z3oXuAg9eqcA4-kjR3Qfgk-hKQ1BOPY4/view?usp=drive_link>

It helps in understanding if the users who detoured were a temporary distraction or break in the journey. Behavior of users who detoured and returned is distinctly different from the one who never left, as they demonstrated intent but got distracted. Recovery rates, analyzed alongside the detour paths give analysts a direct measure of experience resilience, how forgiving is the journey when something goes wrong?

In our analysis, only 38% of the users who detoured recovered back in the journey suggesting a need to enhance the journey experience to improve their resilience.

Along with the 4 lenses, these additional contributors help better understand the customer journey.

### **Cohorts:**

All users do not follow the same path or journey. There are inherent differences between user journeys, it could be caused due to the device they are using, region, gender etc. These differences can be defined as cohorts of the users. Applying these cohorts to slice the aggregate journey at an individual cohort level to identify cohort level 'Progression,' 'Friction,' 'Detour' and 'Recovery'. These lenses give a better understanding of cohort or segment level performance.

### Stage-Level Contributors:

To get additional perspective and insights on each of the lenses at an individual stage, we can identify diagnostic contributors to further analyze the behavior, in my analysis for Ecommerce the following contributors were applied,

* **Pages or Top paths:** It helps in understanding what are the top contributing pages at the stage, these pages play critical influencing role on user’s action, any page which is expected and not present in the list should be analyzed and optimized to improve the performance.

Image 6: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1JZzUCXxIelFJHiy1NmYuaWSLV6n-YJ42/view?usp=drive_link>

* **Channels:** The top traffic source view at the journey stage helps in understanding if the volumes at a particular journey stage is greatly influenced by internal site navigation and finding methods or driven by the marketing campaigns

Image 7: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1x4rQ2eJj3Uzq3XabePaitOcUsHCfYdHf/view?usp=drive_link>

* **Micro-events:** As we already saw at ‘View Item’ stage while understanding Friction, Micro-events are greatly helpful in understanding if the actions performed by the users are aligned directly to the Journey progression or contributing to Friction although still favorable, for instance the ‘Add to Wishlist’ action instead of ‘Add to Cart’ action which drives the real business goal.

### Conclusion

Journey Explorer can represent a meaningful approach for analysts to conduct customer journey analysis. By shifting the unit of analysis from just pages to transitions in the journey, it brings into focus what aggregate metrics have obscured, the actual experience of the users moving through the journey, making decisions, hitting walls, taking detours, and sometimes recovering to find their way back.\
\
\
\
No single lens makes the Journey Explorer powerful. It is the combination of lenses along with cohorts and their contributing factors. Progression establishes the baseline. Friction pinpoints where intervention is needed. Detour reveals the path users take when the designed journey fails them. Recovery measures how well the experience holds under real-world conditions. Cohort filtering ensures these insights are specific rather than averaged across all users, because behavior that looks uniform at aggregate data level is rarely uniform in practice.

For analysts, Journey Explorer is an additional layer and not a replacement for dashboards, it answers the questions dashboards surface most of the time, but cannot resolve them independently. When a KPI drops, Journey Explorer tells us where the drop originated from in the journey, what did the users do instead, and whether the experience recovered. That is the difference between monitoring performance and understanding the journey.

*Imagine you have to meet your CMO in next half-an hour and you know that you have to give a compelling reason and strategy to correct the 18% week over week drop in conversions. As a quick go to tool, you opened the performance dashboard and vigorously tried to slice KPIs by device, channels, geography but you were still unable to find any compelling reason for 'why conversions declined'?*

KPIs such as bounce rates, sessions, pageviews were the foundation of a digital dashboard for nearly two decades. In general, KPIs are used according to the context of the analysis to measure and report performance. However, they only help us understand ‘what happened’ and not so much to understand ‘why users behave in certain ways or how business outcomes can be achieved.’ As customer journeys have become increasingly complex and businesses are expected to deliver consistent performance improvements every passing quarter or year, the insights expected from data have increased in both volume and complexity. Analysts are expected to answer questions such as,

* Why did conversions drop despite an increase in traffic?

* Why do users abandon their carts even after adding shipping information?

* Why do our most loyal customers suddenly churn?

The question ‘why’ is deceptively simple and greatly influences the business and marketing strategy, but deriving meaningful insights is difficult. The aggregated metrics, KPIs, and dimension combinations that signal health, often hide important behavioral nuances needed to understand and interpret the ‘why.’ The problem is not with the dashboarding platforms or the concept of KPIs. The questions business stakeholders ask have simply evolved beyond what dashboards can answer.

To effectively address business concerns and keep pace with evolving customer expectations, especially when Customer Experience (CX) has become far more fragmented across diverse channels, devices, and asynchronous interactions, the Analytics practice should evolve to look beyond traditional KPIs and structured dashboards toward a journey-centric analysis approach, I call this approach the Journey Explorer. Here is a working reference of Journey Explorer implemented for an Ecommerce site, it can be explored at <https://devyendarm.github.io/JourneyExplorer>.

Journey Analysis as a concept is not new. Traditional analysis was focused only on ‘Progression’ with Funnel and Flow visualization, ignoring the critical causal factors of the journey, such as 'Friction,' 'Detour,' and 'Recovery'. Let’s look at how applying these lenses on a simple Customer Journey for an Ecommerce site helps in getting better insights.

### **1. Progression: **It focuses on **“**Do** **users move forward”.

Image 1: [https://drive.google.com/file/d/17wvnnJux1ZXbe-dC_WdOanmgwy7NrXFQ](https://drive.google.com/file/d/17wvnnJux1ZXbe-dC_WdOanmgwy7NrXFQ/edit)

It tells us whether users are moving forward in the journey as designed or there are stages where users fall off the journey the most, signaling friction at that journey stage, hampering users to move forward in the expected journey flow.

In the Ecommerce site analysis, this was at ‘View Item’ stage, the progression is only 66%, effectively dropping over 30% of users in the journey, clearly there is friction at this stage which is affecting the user’s progression. On the contrary, ‘Add Shipping’ stage has the lowest friction with only 10% of users who added their shipping information ended up not Purchasing, demonstrating high Progression rate, clear indication that designed experience is aligned with the user intent.

### 2. **Friction: **It focuses on “Where do Users stall?”.

Image 2: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1LaTEHs3hbnglmwUz0t4_J6k2aJTLBOdu/view?usp=drive_link>

Unlike bounce rates and exit rates, which tell us only where someone left the site, friction analysis tells us where the user hesitated, how long they stayed stuck, and whether they attempted the step multiple times before giving up.

As we saw in our Journey Explorer analysis, friction exists at ‘View Item’ stage related to Product View on the Ecommerce site. There can be many reasons for friction that need to be diagnosed. The diagnosis can be effectively done by applying Micro-events, Time factor and other data slicing aspects depending on the context of Analysis.

Image 3: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1GQLBYUjfqxEf-G_ma0Y30FfI6SUJcwBN/view?usp=drive_link>

In our analysis the friction was contributed by the errors and time taken by users to complete the action, which can be seen in the Time distribution chart. The reason for longer time to action can be analyzed with Channel, Page, and Micro-Events performed at this particular stage. On further analysis, Micro-events diagnosis helps us understand that most users chose ‘Add items to Wishlist’ rather than ‘Add to Cart’, although this is a favorable action, it is not aligned to direct desired Journey Progression.

These are the actionable challenges that need to be resolved to remove friction in the user’s journey.

 

### 3. **Detour: **It focuses on “Where do Users go when they get lost?”.

Image 4: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1GF04Q_bKkezTBLPtYRLxtqP68wLWp28Y/view?usp=drive_link>

It is perhaps one of the most underutilized insights in traditional analytics. When users are not proceeding forward in the journey, they either exit the site or take a detour to some other pages which are not aligned with the desired journey. Detour analysis helps in mapping those alternate paths, revealing whether the users are seeking help, second-guessing their intent, or working around a broken experience. The detour paths often expose UX gaps that surveys or heatmaps rarely surface.

In our analysis, we see users browsing FAQ and Shipping Policy pages at the critical Checkout process, although volume is low, these detours could lead to Abandoned Cart journeys.

### 4. **Recovery: **It focuses on “Do Users come back?”.

Image 5: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1z3oXuAg9eqcA4-kjR3Qfgk-hKQ1BOPY4/view?usp=drive_link>

It helps in understanding if the users who detoured were due to a temporary distraction or break in the journey. Behavior of users who detoured and returned is distinctly different from the one who never left, as they demonstrated intent but got distracted. Recovery rates, analyzed alongside the detour paths, give analysts a direct measure of experience resilience and how forgiving is the journey when something goes wrong.

In our analysis, only 38% of the users who detoured recovered back in the journey suggesting a need to enhance the journey experience to improve their resilience.

### **Additional Contributors for Diagnosis:**

Along with the 4 lenses, there can be many contributors that help slice lenses of the customer journey.

These can be:

* **Segments: **All users do not follow the same path or journey. There are inherent differences between users, it could be the device they use, their region or gender etc. These differences can be defined as segments of users. Applying these segments to slice the aggregate journey at a specific user group level helps in isolating the noise and distribution buried under averaged or aggregated view. The specific segment level customer journey provides better understanding of the journey performance when analyzed with the lenses of 'Progression,' 'Friction,' 'Detour' and 'Recovery'.

* **Specific Stage-Level:** These are stage-specific diagnostic contributors, in my analysis for Ecommerce the following contributors were applied,

  * **Pages or Top paths:** It helped in understanding what are the top contributing pages at the given stage, these pages played a critical influencing role on user’s action, any page which was expected and not present in the list should be analyzed and optimized to improve the performance.

Image 6: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1JZzUCXxIelFJHiy1NmYuaWSLV6n-YJ42/view?usp=drive_link>

* **Channels:** The top traffic source view at the journey stage helped in understanding if the volumes at a particular journey stage are greatly influenced by internal site navigation and finding methods or driven by the marketing campaigns.

Image 7: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1x4rQ2eJj3Uzq3XabePaitOcUsHCfYdHf/view?usp=drive_link>

* **Micro-events:** As we already saw at ‘View Item’ stage while understanding Friction, Micro-events were greatly helpful in understanding if the actions performed by the users are aligned directly to the Journey progression or contributing to Friction, for instance the ‘Add to Wishlist’ action although favorable did not directly influence the journey progression similar to ‘Add to Cart’ action which drives the real business goal.

* Why did conversions drop despite an increase in traffic?

* Why do users abandon their carts even after adding shipping information?

* Why do our most loyal customers suddenly churn?

The question ‘why’ is deceptively simple and greatly influences the business and marketing strategy, but deriving meaningful insights is difficult. The aggregated metrics, KPIs, and dimension combinations that signal health, often hide important behavioral nuances needed to understand and interpret the ‘why.’ The problem is not with the dashboarding platforms or the concept of KPIs. The questions business stakeholders ask have simply evolved beyond what dashboards can answer.

To effectively address business concerns and keep pace with evolving customer expectations, especially when Customer Experience (CX) has become far more fragmented across diverse channels, devices, and asynchronous interactions, the Analytics practice should evolve to look beyond traditional KPIs and structured dashboards toward a journey-centric analysis approach, I call this approach the Journey Explorer. Here is a working reference of Journey Explorer implemented for an Ecommerce site, it can be explored at <https://devyendarm.github.io/JourneyExplorer>.

Journey Analysis as a concept is not new. Traditional analysis was focused only on ‘Progression’ with Funnel and Flow visualization, ignoring the critical causal factors of the journey, such as 'Friction,' 'Detour,' and 'Recovery'. Let’s look at how applying these lenses on a simple Customer Journey for an Ecommerce site helps in getting better insights.

### **1. Progression: **It focuses on **“**Do** **users move forward”.

Image 1: [https://drive.google.com/file/d/17wvnnJux1ZXbe-dC_WdOanmgwy7NrXFQ](https://drive.google.com/file/d/17wvnnJux1ZXbe-dC_WdOanmgwy7NrXFQ/edit)

It tells us whether users are moving forward in the journey as designed or there are stages where users fall off the journey the most, signaling friction at that journey stage, hampering users to move forward in the expected journey flow.

In the Ecommerce site analysis, this was at ‘View Item’ stage, the progression is only 66%, effectively dropping over 30% of users in the journey, clearly there is friction at this stage which is affecting the user’s progression. On the contrary, ‘Add Shipping’ stage has the lowest friction with only 10% of users who added their shipping information ended up not Purchasing, demonstrating high Progression rate, clear indication that designed experience is aligned with the user intent.

### 2. **Friction: **It focuses on “Where do Users stall?”.

Image 2: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1LaTEHs3hbnglmwUz0t4_J6k2aJTLBOdu/view?usp=drive_link>

Unlike bounce rates and exit rates, which tell us only where someone left the site, friction analysis tells us where the user hesitated, how long they stayed stuck, and whether they attempted the step multiple times before giving up.

As we saw in our Journey Explorer analysis, friction exists at ‘View Item’ stage related to Product View on the Ecommerce site. There can be many reasons for friction that need to be diagnosed. The diagnosis can be effectively done by applying Micro-events, Time factor and other data slicing aspects depending on the context of Analysis.

Image 3: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1GQLBYUjfqxEf-G_ma0Y30FfI6SUJcwBN/view?usp=drive_link>

In our analysis the friction was contributed by the errors and time taken by users to complete the action, which can be seen in the Time distribution chart. The reason for longer time to action can be analyzed with Channel, Page, and Micro-Events performed at this particular stage. On further analysis, Micro-events diagnosis helps us understand that most users chose ‘Add items to Wishlist’ rather than ‘Add to Cart’, although this is a favorable action, it is not aligned to direct desired Journey Progression.

These are the actionable challenges that need to be resolved to remove friction in the user’s journey.

 

### 3. **Detour: **It focuses on “Where do Users go when they get lost?”.

Image 4: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1GF04Q_bKkezTBLPtYRLxtqP68wLWp28Y/view?usp=drive_link>

It is perhaps one of the most underutilized insights in traditional analytics. When users are not proceeding forward in the journey, they either exit the site or take a detour to some other pages which are not aligned with the desired journey. Detour analysis helps in mapping those alternate paths, revealing whether the users are seeking help, second-guessing their intent, or working around a broken experience. The detour paths often expose UX gaps that surveys or heatmaps rarely surface.

In our analysis, we see users browsing FAQ and Shipping Policy pages at the critical Checkout process, although volume is low, these detours could lead to Abandoned Cart journeys.

### 4. **Recovery: **It focuses on “Do Users come back?”.

Image 5: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1z3oXuAg9eqcA4-kjR3Qfgk-hKQ1BOPY4/view?usp=drive_link>

It helps in understanding if the users who detoured were due to a temporary distraction or break in the journey. Behavior of users who detoured and returned is distinctly different from the one who never left, as they demonstrated intent but got distracted. Recovery rates, analyzed alongside the detour paths, give analysts a direct measure of experience resilience and how forgiving the journey is when something goes wrong.

In our analysis, only 38% of the users who detoured recovered back in the journey suggesting a need to enhance the journey experience to improve their resilience.

### **Additional Contributors for Diagnosis:**

Along with the 4 lenses, there can be many contributors that help slice lenses of the customer journey.

These can be:

* **Segments: **All users do not follow the same path or journey. There are inherent differences between users, it could be the device they use, their region or gender etc. These differences can be defined as segments of users. Applying these segments to slice the aggregate journey at a specific user group level helps in isolating the noise and distribution buried under averaged or aggregated view. The specific segment level customer journey provides better understanding of the journey performance when analyzed with the lenses of 'Progression,' 'Friction,' 'Detour' and 'Recovery'.

* **Specific Stage-Level:** These are stage-specific diagnostic contributors, in my analysis for Ecommerce the following contributors were applied,

  * **Pages or Top paths:** It helped in understanding what are the top contributing pages at the given stage, these pages played a critical influencing role on user’s action, any page which was expected and not present in the list should be analyzed and optimized to improve the performance.

Image 6: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1JZzUCXxIelFJHiy1NmYuaWSLV6n-YJ42/view?usp=drive_link>

* **Channels:** The top traffic source view at the journey stage helped in understanding if the volumes at a particular journey stage are greatly influenced by internal site navigation and finding methods or driven by the marketing campaigns.

Image 7: <https://drive.google.com/file/d/1x4rQ2eJj3Uzq3XabePaitOcUsHCfYdHf/view?usp=drive_link>

* **Micro-events:** As we already saw at ‘View Item’ stage while understanding Friction, Micro-events were greatly helpful in understanding if the actions performed by the users are aligned directly to the Journey progression or contributing to Friction, for instance the ‘Add to Wishlist’ action although favorable did not directly influence the journey progression similar to ‘Add to Cart’ action which drives the real business goal.

### Conclusion

Journey Explorer can represent a meaningful approach for analysts to conduct customer journey analysis. By shifting the unit of analysis from just pages to transitions in the journey, it brings into focus what aggregate metrics have obscured, the actual experience of the users moving through the journey, making decisions, hitting walls, taking detours, and sometimes recovering to find their way back.\
No single lens makes the Journey Explorer powerful. It is the combination of lenses along with their contributing factors. Progression establishes the baseline. Friction pinpoints where intervention is needed. Detour reveals the path users take when the designed journey fails them. Recovery measures how well the experience holds under real-world conditions. Segment filtering ensures these insights are specific rather than averaged across all users, because behavior that looks uniform at aggregate data level is rarely uniform in practice.

For analysts, Journey Explorer is an additional layer and not a replacement for dashboards, it answers the questions dashboards surface most of the time, but cannot resolve them independently. When a KPI drops, Journey Explorer tells us where the drop originated from in the journey, what did the users do instead, and whether the experience recovered. That is the difference between monitoring performance and understanding the journey.
